speaker: interviewer
text: No, to identify [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: You're not sweating through that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Brenda Senders, tape four.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I still have a sip?

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have what you want.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK--

speaker: interviewee
text: So mine--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to finish up your story. And then we're going to put your-- use your photographs, your, your, your pictures. And it'll be a nice tape. It'll be a good tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Be a good tape? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You're doing good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue with your story, Brenda. You, you went over into Czechoslovakia-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- yes, we went over to Czechoslovakia, clandestinely over the border. And from Czechoslovakia, we slept on the-- something was done there, some kind of-- I don't know. Some kinda union or some kinda understanding, you understand, between the Bricha then there, the, the Jewish-- whatever, the Bricha and they were working together. We went over. And from there we came to Austria. And in Austria, I wind up to be in a kibbutz, in Braunau am Inn. This is the ironic thing-- in the place where Hitler was born. And then there was a kibbutz. And there I met Leyah, my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were married in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Austria, in Braunau am Inn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: And next, we went to Italy. He find his sister. He just find out-- the day we got married, he had some news that his sister survived in a concentration camp from Bergen-Belsen, and she's in Italy. So we came, and we met up with her, his sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: But he had to-- he was working with the Haganah at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sister make it through to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, yes, wind up on the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: The famous boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: The famous, what Leon Uris wrote his book. I have pictures given from the, from the, what my sister gave me. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and she, she went on the They came to Palestine at that time, to the outskirts of Haifa. And they saw the people were waving to them. And they waved to the people. But the English didn't let them to get off. They brought them back to Cyprus. They brought them to Marseilles, to France. And on there, the people of the boat didn't want to embark. They said we are going to die with the boat. And frogs came down under the boat, and they were ready to, to, to, to make a end to it. And the English at that time were very afraid of public opinion and all this and stories. And they made a deal with the Bricha. They were led off the boat of the people was taken to Hamburg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they took them from Hamburg, and through the [NON-ENGLISH] Bricha, they made a deal. They brought them to Hamburg. They, they disembarked the people. And then later on, they went, they went to-- to Palestine at that time. But then I find out, while being in Italy, that's my sister. I never had no connection. I didn't know where and what. At that time I didn't know, not I know she was on a boat called And, and she's someplace in, near Hamburg, near Hamburg. And it was a tough thing to, to, to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided that I have to bring my sister to be with me. I went to the Polish consular. I made a passport. And he said, this was unique, the only one he ever made. I said, made it, make it. I, I, I insisted, and he did it, with my sister on the passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: Going down there, they stamped out. They did not pay attention. Coming back, I told them that she went with a, with a, with a bunch of kids. You know, that's my, my story. I-- I got my passport. I, I went off in Germany. I took, I took assumed name. They gave me-- I went to a special-- was group, to, to, they were the Beitar. The Beitar, they were the right wing at that time. And I went, and I-- they already told them that I'm coming-- that's my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell him. --that I'm coming down there. And they had a name for me, a assumed name, they gave me. The profile looked like me. And I had to memorize her name and year of birth. And from them point-- from that, from that point on, I had to go from Munich-- and I had a disaster coming back to Munich, and this is another story. I was arrested by the MP. And I almost was-- was brought to the Czechoslovakian border. I begged myself out. And then I came back to Munich. I got this passport, this, this, this assumed passport. I went through three zones. I went the American zone, the English and the French. Remember, Germany was split. I had to go through those zones, come to, to-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] to Hamburg. Sh. Come to Hamburg. And from down there, they were in the-- some place away. And it was not near Hamburg. It was about 15, 20 kilometers from Hamburg. I have to go to the, to the UNRRA, I remember, or to the giant, to giant distribution committee. And from them I find out where-- I told them that I am also belong. Because they wouldn't, they wouldn't let me in. I told them that I belong also on the, on this, on this, on the kibbutz down there, with the, with the boat people, with the, with the, with the people. And it was tricky. But I did it. I went into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my sister, in the beginning, she said, yes, she's coming with me. All of a sudden, her, her supervisor, her madrich, who was a, a head-- you know her-- [NON-ENGLISH], how do you say it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Her counselor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her counselor, yes, exactly. Her counselor start talking, what are you going, this, this-- start propagating her. And she came out, she said she's not coming with me. Here all the efforts I made. I almost fainted, I remember. And to the end, she decided to come with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Now, your uncle, did he also go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle, yes. He went to Palestine, he-- with his son, [? Dolph ?]. He recently passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: About two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you and your husband, then, are now in Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you come to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Italy, remember, there was the Truman's-- the 100,000 refugees they let in. We came on this quota. I had relatives in Washington. I had my mother's sister. Fanny was her name, Fanny Goring. And I find her through my uncle, told me, gave me all the information. Through a student and through the police department, I find out where she was. And she send us affidavit. And that's how we wind up to be in Washington. And what year was that? In 1951, in the end. You can call it 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Christmas, I remember, we were celebrating on the, on the ship. And we are [INAUDIBLE] already in the states.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you and your husband, then, continue to live in Washington?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Up-- till--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what business was your-- were you and your husband in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was office machines.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you move to Florida?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Florida, we moved six years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're retired now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a remarkable story--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, I'm still doing--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Brenda, and I want to thank you for sharing it with us. Now we'd-- now we'd like to see some of your pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope I said it [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: You did very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for listening to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this picture was taken in Poland, I would say approximately 1924. My mother-- this is my mother, my father. [POINTING] That's me, when I was once a little girl. That was my aunt Rosa, my uncle Glazer-- [NON-ENGLISH] we called him. This is my grandmother, who perished during the Holocaust. This is my great-grandmother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my aunt Chaya. And this is my uncle Ruben. And I call him my favorite uncle. And this is when a German threw a piece of bread to him. He didn't pick it up, because-- This is my grandfather-- his mother, my great-grandmother. This is my aunt Devorah. She left Poland to Palestine. She, from all the people, remained alive. That's my uncle, me, and my aunt Devorah, who left for Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, OK, this is my mother. And the picture was taken in Poland, what year, I don't know. My mother was young at that time. It looks like 1919, 1920, I would say it. That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, and that's me, right before I get my gun as a partisan. And this is 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is a picture was taken in Poland. That was hard before the war. I, I, I don't-- this is I find through my relatives in Pal-- in Israel, when I went for a visit. They had it. This is approximately '39, I want to say-- '39, I would call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture is of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of me, yes. This is a picture of me in my school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture that was taken I would say in Poland. And this is my grandmother, Sarah, my mother, Leah. And this is my aunt Devorah, who went to Palestine. And she passed away recently.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is the passport. I was-- it was made in Milano. It was given by the counselor in Milano, the Polish counselor, to me. And I insisted that my sister remained alive, and I must bring her from Germany to Italy, back. And this is the first of a kind, he said. He made it. But he did. I insisted, and I succeeded for him to do it. [POINTING] And this is my sister and myself. And that's how I brought her back from Germany to Italy. I could bring her in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is the picture of us taken right here in Florida. My-- our daughter-- this is my husband. [POINTING] This is me. This is our son-in-law, Phil. This is our daughter, Rita. And this is our son, son, Stanley. This I think three years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, to the extreme left is our son-in-law, Mark. [POINTING] Next to him, sitting, is Leyah, my husband, our granddaughter, Rochelle. Rochelle. There is me, Brenda. On the second line is Justin, our son, son's little boy. This is our daughter Sherry and her little girl, Laura. And this is Rita, and our son-in-law, Phil. Stanley's not in it. But he's on the other picture.
